focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

She checked the phone again and caught her own blurred face in the black screen: damp hair, old T-shirt, the strip of space between the bed and the stacked storage bins behind her. The fan in the window pushed hot air across Lena's desk instead of out, clicking every ninth turn. She had showered anyway because there was only so much of other people's sauce and the walk home and her own skin she could stand carrying. The brick had stopped radiating and started merely holding the day's heat. The apartment still felt used up by it.

She knew her angles in the divorced-woman way that had nothing to do with vanity and everything to do with damage control. She kept her chin tipped and stole window light if she could. She showed nothing from below and nothing full-length unless the dress had structure enough to lie for her. Her Hinge pictures gave Eli a good face, good collarbones, one wedding-guest dress from two years ago that still suggested a waist if you caught it from the right side. They did not give him the soft lower belly that folded when she sat, or the pale pressure marks a bra left under her breasts, or the little scar on her left knee that looked harsher on camera than it did in life. They did not give him the body the last year of marriage had turned into a lighting problem.

Signal lit up with his name.

You home?

Then, before she could answer:

Call me when you're alone.

And then, because he had a good sense for exactly how much to push:

I've been thinking about you since your brother made me say grace over speakerphone.

She smiled before she meant to. Gabe had not actually made him say grace. Gabe had asked if theater boys knew any prayers not written by dead Irishmen, and Eli had laughed and said he knew one for overcooked chicken. The twins...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...t woman behind his apartment and the kid on Lena's block who kept practicing wheelies badly enough that every attempt ended with a metallic slam and an adult swearing. He asked her to put the phone near the window for a second so he could hear the buses and scooters. She did. He said he liked cities best at the hour when everybody who meant trouble was already either committed to it or asleep.

When he yawned she heard it all the way through, not polite, jaw-cracking, body-first. It made her tenderness for him feel indecently domestic.

"Go to bed," she said.

"You first."

"I'm already in bed."

"Then close your eyes."

"That's too intimate."

"You paused the app."

"You're insufferable."

"And yet."

She did close her eyes because it let his voice move closer.

When they finally hung up, the rush transcript was still open on her laptop, untouched for an hour. The time in the corner said 1:43. The ugliest section was still half-marked, and her client wanted the cleaned file by eight.

She got out of bed, crossed to the desk, and read three lines without taking in a word. Then she opened email instead.

"Running into an export glitch on my end. I'll have the rush file to you by nine," she wrote, and sent it before she could revise the lie into something nobler. It might cost her part of the invoice. It might cost her the next rush if the client got tired of excuses. Relief came first anyway.

The IRS notice was still under the cable on her desk. The fan still clicked every ninth turn.

She picked the phone back up, turned the volume to loud, and opened her alarm app.

He had said he would be awake before call time and probably free for ten minutes around seven.

She set the alarm twenty minutes earlier than work required and left the phone on the pillow beside her head.
